text: hello and welcome to another session of inguinal hernia and hernia cases today we're going to be talking about management of femoral hernias in women we're going to review a recent case i performed and talk about strategies for effective treatment of femoral hernias this was a thirty nine year old woman with chronic groin discomfort she had a pretty extensive workup which included a full gynecologic workup which demonstrated no abnormalities She was noted to have an infrainguinal bulge with discomfort and pain with activity. An ultrasound showed a bowel containing hernia, likely femoral, with no other surgical history present.

We thought she was a good candidate for minimally invasive repair. Femoral hernias present as two point six percent of all groin hernias and are more commonly found in women. They are between twenty two and fifty three percent of all diagnosed inguinal hernias or groin hernias in women, as opposed to one to eight percent for men. Commonly, these are missed at the time of operation, especially when performing an open inguinal repair with a requirement of up to forty percent of patients requiring a re-operation. One third of femoral hernias require emergency surgery. So the recommendations now are to definitively repair them when identified. The gold standard has recently been modified to be MIS or preperitoneal repair.
The surgeon begins the development of a peritoneal flap, revealing a femoral hernia on the right side and an obturator hernia.

The surgeon observes that the patient's medial ligament is not prominent. The peritoneal flap is initiated approximately one to two centimeters above the arcuate line, which is visible below the starting point of the flap.

The surgeon begins developing the peritoneal flap, moving towards the medial landmarks near the pubis. Cooper's ligament is not identified due to anticipated difficulty in exposure, focusing instead on the initial flap development.

The surgeon performs medial dissection to isolate the femoral hernia, noting tight tissue planes likely due to chronic inflammation. The hernia is visually identified as containing peritoneal invagination without bowel, preparing for reduction.
The surgeon isolates and identifies the femoral hernia, with Cooper's ligament visible posteriorly, the lacunar ligament medially, and the iliopubic tract anteriorly.

The surgeon carefully reduces the femoral hernia, taking care to avoid aggressive dissection near the lateral vein to prevent vascular injury. The obturator hernia is also visible and is being reduced.

The surgeon identifies a fat-containing hernia with peritoneal invagination, which could potentially lead to incarceration or strangulation. The team proceeds with developing the retropubic dissection, a step commonly performed in female patients.
During the preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair, the surgeon sacrifices the round ligament to ensure the mesh can be laid flat without risk of malposition.

The surgeon transects the round ligament close to the peritoneum, taking care to manage potential bleeding. An adequate pocket is developed for mesh placement, with visible iliac vessels, vein, and nerve in the surgical field.

The surgeon positions the mesh over the surgical site, ensuring it is well-covered below Cooper's ligament. Multiple fixation points are applied at Cooper's ligament to prevent mesh migration, allowing for consistent posterior placement and effective coverage of the defects.
The surgeon performs a standard peritoneal flap closure, ensuring there are no holes in the peritoneum, resulting in a straightforward closure.
The surgeon performs a flap closure with reduced insufflation pressure, carefully taking the flaps in two bites to minimize the risk of injury or creating large holes in the tissue.

So there you have it, a pretty straightforward femoral and obturator hernia repair. This patient did really well in the post-operative period. She went home the same day and post-operatively had a dramatic improvement in her groin pain. She went from having debilitating pain to essentially zero pain on post-operative day one. No narcotics used and returned to work on post-operative day two. I did the operation on Saturday and she was back to work on Monday as a school teacher. So great case, great outcome and a nice approach to management.
